No; in FY2022 purchases of property, plant and equipment were $1,749 million versus net sales of $34,229 million (capex ≈5.1% of sales).

Operating margin change in FY2022 was driven by organic growth/productivity (benefits from strong pricing, spending discipline and prior restructuring, partly offset by a 0.3 percentage-point decline from lower disposable respirator demand and manufacturing/geopolitical headwinds) that provided a net benefit, while higher raw material and logistics costs from inflationary pressures (a ~2.4 percentage-point headwind), foreign exchange (~$271 million decrease in operating income), divestiture impacts (lost income and stranded costs), net costs for significant litigation and other expense increases (notably higher non-service pension costs) reduced margins. Special items, including gains on business divestitures and divestiture-related restructuring, also materially affected reported operating margins.

The quick ratio at June 30, 2023 is (cash $4,258 + marketable securities $56 + accounts receivable $4,947) / current liabilities $10,936 ≈ 0.85. While this is below 1, 3M’s MD&A states the Company “maintains a strong liquidity profile” and highlights $4.3 billion of cash and marketable securities, access to a commercial paper program (up to $5.0 billion with $1.8 billion outstanding) and an undrawn $4.25 billion revolving credit facility, so the quick ratio alone may not fully reflect 3M’s liquidity.
None.

Yes. Cash dividends declared and paid were $1.50 and $1.49 per share for the first and second quarters of 2023 and 2022, respectively, totaling $3.00 and $2.98 per share for the first six months of 2023 and 2022, indicating a stable trend.

No — Adobe’s operating margin declined in FY2022 versus FY2021 (operating income $6,098m on revenue $17,606m in 2022 vs $5,802m on $15,785m in 2021), decreasing from about 36.8% to about 34.6%. The report notes cost of revenue increased 16% and operating expenses increased 15% in fiscal 2022, which grew faster than revenue and compressed operating margins.

Yes — cash flows from operations increased to $7.84 billion in fiscal 2022 from $7.23 billion in fiscal 2021 while net income declined slightly to $4,756 million, indicating an improving free cash flow conversion as of FY2022.

Approximately 12.1 times (Inventory turnover = $10,069 million ÷ (($1,055 million + $604 million)/2) ≈ 12.1).  
Inventories comprise fuel and other raw materials ($733) and spare parts and supplies ($322) as of December 31, 2022, so a conventional inventory turnover calculation is meaningful.

The filing reported that on June 30, 2022 Amcor Finance (USA), Inc. and Amcor Flexibles North America, Inc. entered into First and Second Supplemental Indentures to effect the substitution of the Substitute Issuer for the Former Issuer and the assumption by the Substitute Issuer of the Former Issuer’s covenants under the indentures governing the 2026 and 2028 notes.
AMCOR's quick ratio improved from approximately 0.53 in FY2022 to approximately 0.57 in FY2023.

FY2023 — Aug 1, 2022: acquired 100% equity interest in a Czech Republic flexible packaging manufacturing plant for $59 million; Mar 17, 2023: acquired 100% equity interest in a medical device packaging site in Shanghai, China for $60 million (including $20 million contingent); May 31, 2023: acquired a New Zealand automated protein packaging machines manufacturer for $45 million (including $13 million contingent). FY2022 — No acquisitions are described; the Company completed the disposal of non-core assets in the Flexibles reporting segment. FY2021 — No acquisitions are described; the Company completed disposals of non-core businesses in Europe, India and Argentina and sold its equity investment in AMVIG.
Amcor primarily operates in the packaging industry, developing and producing responsible packaging for food, beverage, pharmaceutical, medical, home and personal-care products.

No—gross profit as a percentage of net sales decreased to 18.5% in FY2023 from 19.4% in FY2022. The company states gross profit decreased $95 million and that the lower gross profit percentage was mainly from the pass-through of higher raw material costs and the impact of disposed operations.

As of December 31, 2022 the restructuring liability represents accruals for employee costs, fixed asset‑related costs and other costs to cover the Company’s restructuring activities (primarily related to the Russia‑Ukraine conflict and the 2019 Bemis integration), with a $93 million balance; the majority of these liabilities are expected to be paid within the next twelve months and are recorded on the balance sheet under other current liabilities and other non‑current liabilities.

Yes — AMD's quick ratio for FY22 is approximately 1.77 ((15,019 − 3,771) / 6,369), and AMD states its cash, cash equivalents, short-term investments and credit facilities will be sufficient to fund operations over the next 12 months.

Server microprocessors (CPUs) and graphics processing units (GPUs), data processing units (DPUs), Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs), and Adaptive System-on-Chip (SoC) products for data centers; CPUs, accelerated processing units (APUs) that integrate CPUs and GPUs, and chipsets for desktop and notebook personal computers; discrete GPUs, semi-custom SoC products and development services; embedded CPUs, GPUs, APUs, FPGAs, and Adaptive SoC products; and, from time to time, sales or licenses of portions of AMD’s intellectual property (IP) portfolio.

The increase was driven by a 64% increase in Data Center revenue primarily due to higher sales of our EPYC™ server processors, a 21% increase in Gaming revenue primarily due to higher semi‑custom product sales, and a significant increase in Embedded revenue driven by the inclusion of Xilinx embedded product sales; this growth was partially offset by a 10% decrease in Client revenue primarily due to lower processor shipments driven by a weak PC market and significant inventory correction actions across the PC supply chain.

The decline in operating margin in FY22 was driven primarily by amortization of acquisition-related intangible assets from the Xilinx and Pensando acquisitions—which reduced gross margin (gross margin 45% in 2022 vs 48% in 2021, the decrease primarily due to amortization of intangible assets associated with the Xilinx acquisition) and added $1.4 billion to cost of sales and $2.1 billion to operating expense—together with higher research and development expense ($5,005m vs $2,845m) and higher marketing, general and administrative expense ($2,336m vs $1,448m).
Operating activities provided $3.6 billion of net cash in 2022.
Data Center segment.

United States; EMEA (Europe, the Middle East and Africa); APAC (Asia Pacific, Australia and New Zealand); and LACC (Latin America, Canada and the Caribbean).

No — Amex’s pretax margin declined to about 18.1% in 2022 (9,585/52,862) from about 25.2% in 2021 (10,689/42,380). Operating margin can be a less useful metric for American Express because provisions for credit losses are large and volatile (2,182 in 2022 versus (1,419) in 2021) and the company emphasizes metrics such as net interest yield and return on average equity.

Gross margin is not a useful metric for American Express because the company reports and manages performance using “Total revenues net of interest expense” and metrics such as net interest yield on average Card Member loans and network volumes; its profitability is driven by revenue mix (discount revenue, net card fees, service fees and processed revenue) and items such as provisions for credit losses, card member rewards, business development and other operating expenses.
Decreased by 3.0 percentage points.

Yes, Card Member retention remained high during 2022.

No — as of December 31, 2022 American Water Works reported total current assets of $1,250 million and total current liabilities of $2,811 million, yielding negative working capital of $1,561 million.

Yes — Best Buy’s consolidated gross profit as a % of revenue was 22.4% in 2021, 22.5% in 2022 and 21.4% in 2023, so consolidated margins fluctuated by less than about 2% year-to-year. However, the International segment’s gross profit % moved from 21.5% in 2021 to 23.9% in 2022 (a 2.4% change), which exceeds the ~2% threshold.

Fiscal 2023: none disclosed. Fiscal 2022: Current Health Ltd. (acquired November 2, 2021 for net cash consideration of $389 million) and Two Peaks, LLC d/b/a Yardbird Furniture (acquired November 4, 2021 for net cash consideration of $79 million). Fiscal 2021: none disclosed.

Yes — Commercial Airplanes ($25,867 of $66,608), Defense, Space & Security ($23,162 of $66,608), and Global Services ($17,611 of $66,608) each represent more than 20% of Boeing’s 2022 revenues.

Yes; Note 21 discloses multiple pending legal proceedings including actions related to the October 29, 2018 Lion Air and March 10, 2019 Ethiopian Airlines 737 MAX accidents (including an SEC settlement in September 2022 resulting in an earnings charge of $200 paid in October 2022 and a shareholder derivative settlement that resulted in the Company receiving $219 in the second quarter of 2022) and an ongoing arbitration with Embraer over termination of joint venture agreements.

Yes — Cost of sales as a percentage of Revenues decreased from 95.2% in 2021 to 94.7% in 2022, indicating a slight improvement in Boeing’s gross margin profile in FY2022. Interpretation is limited because the BCA segment predominantly uses program accounting and accounting quantities (which the company says are a key determinant of the gross margins recognized) and many BDS/BGS contracts are long‑term, so cost of sales and gross margins are significantly affected by program/accounting estimates and contract accounting methods.
Boeing’s primary customers are the commercial airline industry worldwide and defense/government customers, including U.S. government agencies and the branches of the U.S. military.

Yes — Boeing states it depends heavily on commercial airlines and that historically the airline industry has been cyclical, which can result in fewer new orders and lower revenues, profitability and cash flows.

They expect to gradually return 787 production to 5 per month in 2023, to implement further gradual increases to 737 production beyond the 31-per-month rate set in 2022 based on market demand and supply chain capacity, and to resume 777X production in 2023 after the production pause.
Boeing's effective tax rate in FY2022 was (0.6)% compared with 14.7% in FY2021.
The effective tax rate increased by 2.7 percentage points, from 20.2% in FY2021 to 22.9% in FY2022.
Yes; Corning reported positive working capital of $2,278 million as of December 31, 2022, with a current ratio of 1.4:1.
